fact: Los Angeles built the St. Francis Dam to store water conveyed from the Owens Valley, roughly 230 miles away (the script says 'four hundred miles').
source: Floodpath: The Deadliest Man-Made Disaster of 20th-Century America and the Making of Modern Los Angeles, Jon Wilkerson (2016); also Wikipedia

fact: William Mulholland designed the St. Francis Dam essentially alone, without independent engineering review.
source: Floodpath, Jon Wilkerson (2016); also J. David Rogers, 'Lessons Learned from the St. Francis Dam Failure,' GeoStrata, 2006

fact: The eastern canyon wall (the dam's east abutment) was composed of ancient landslide deposits and a paleomegalandslide schist formation that was unstable when saturated.
source: J. David Rogers, 'Reassessment of the St. Francis Dam Failure,' Proceedings, ASDSO Western Regional Conference, 1995

fact: Muddy water and leaks were observed at the base of the dam in the weeks before the collapse; Mulholland personally inspected the dam on the morning of March 12, 1928, and declared the leakage normal.
source: Floodpath, Jon Wilkerson (2016); Los Angeles Coroner's Inquest testimony, 1928; Wikipedia

fact: The dam failed just before midnight on March 12, 1928, killing over 400 people (estimates range from about 411 to over 600).
source: J. David Rogers, 'A Man, A Dam, and A Disaster,' Southern California Quarterly, 1995; also Wikipedia

fact: Mulholland was solely responsible for approving the dam site, its design, and the assessment of the leaks — he held extraordinary unilateral authority as head of the Los Angeles Bureau of Water Works and Supply.
source: Rivers in the Desert: William Mulholland and the Inventing of Los Angeles, Margaret Leslie Davis (1993)
